label: yes
cot_prefix: Okay, so I need to figure out if an 8x8 chessboard with two diagonally opposite corners removed can be completely tiled with 31 dominoes. Each domino covers two adjacent squares. Let me start by recalling what a standard chessboard looks like. A regular 8x8 chessboard has 64 squares, alternating between black and white. If we remove two diagonally opposite corners, those squares must be the same color, right? Because on a chessboard, diagonally opposite corners are the same color. Let me check that: if you take the top-left corner, which is typically a dark square, the diagonally opposite corner would be the bottom-right, which is also dark. So removing both would leave 62 squares, with 32 of one color and 30 of the other. Now, dominoes cover two adjacent squares. Since adjacent squares on a chessboard are always of opposite colors, each domino must cover one black and one white square. Therefore, if we have 31 dominoes, they would need to cover 31 black and 31 white squares. But if the board now has 32 of one color and 30 of the other, that's an imbalance. There's no way to cover 32 and 30 with equal numbers. Therefore, it's impossible. Wait, let me make sure I didn't skip a step. So original chessboard: 32 black, 32 white. Remove two corners of the same color, say two black. Now, 30 black and 32 white? Or wait, removing two squares of the same color would result in 32 - 2 = 30 of that color and 32 of the other? Wait no, if you remove two black squares, then you have 32 - 2 = 30 black and 32 white. So difference of two. Therefore, dominoes each take one black and one white. So 31 dominoes would take 31 black and 31 white. But there are 30 black squares. Therefore, you can't do it. So answer is no. But let me think again. Maybe there's a different way? Maybe the coloring argument isn't the only one? But I remember that in domino tiling problems, the coloring argument is fundamental because dominoes have to cover both colors. Since the two removed squares are same color, you can't tile. So answer is no. But let me check online... Wait, no, I should try to think through.
